planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring; Japanese forest grass grows slowly and is most effective in mass plantings or as an accent against dark stones or deep-shade groundcovers.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Japanese forest grass is one of the finest foliage plants for shaded sites, forming graceful, cascading mounds of arching, golden-yellow or green-and-gold-striped leaves that move elegantly in the slightest breeze and glow with captured light even in deep shade. The golden-variegated 'Aureola' is the most popular cultivar. Avoid hot afternoon sun, which bleaches and burns the foliage; plants perform best in the dappled light of high deciduous canopy or on the north or east side of buildings. Growth is slow but the plant is very long-lived once established. Divide in early spring every 4–5 years to reinvigorate.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring; goat's beard is a large perennial that needs substantial space — allow 4–6 feet of spread at maturity.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade, giving each plant a full 4–5 feet of space; goat's beard is a dramatic, native, large-scale shade perennial whose huge plumes of creamy-white feathery flowers in late spring to early summer rise on stems to 6 feet, mimicking an enormous astilbe or a white smoke tree. Male plants produce the showiest flower plumes; female plants bear smaller plumes and later develop attractive seed clusters. Foliage remains handsome through summer and fall. Excellent at woodland edges, along streams, or in naturalistic shade gardens where its scale can be fully appreciated. Divide only when absolutely necessary as established plants are extremely long-lived and resent disturbance.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; rodgersia thrives at pond margins, along streams, and in boggy shade where soil stays consistently moist.

directions: Plant in moist to permanently wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade near ponds, streams, or low spots that stay consistently moist; rodgersia is a bold architectural shade perennial from East Asia whose large, horse-chestnut-like compound leaves with prominent bronze-tinged veining create a striking textural contrast with finer-textured ferns and hostas. Tall panicles of small white or pink astilbe-like flowers appear in midsummer above the dramatic foliage. Dry soil causes rapid wilting and tip burn — consistent moisture is non-negotiable. Protect new growth from late spring frosts with horticultural fleece as the emerging bronze shoots are vulnerable. Established clumps are very long-lived; divide every 5–8 years in spring.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall without covering for spring germination, or divide established clumps in early fall; wood poppy self-sows freely to form colonies in shaded, moist woodland garden conditions.

directions: Direct sow fresh seed on the soil surface in fall under deciduous trees in moist, humus-rich, well-drained acidic soil; wood poppy (celandine poppy) is a native Eastern North American shade wildflower that produces bright golden-yellow, 4-petaled flowers from spring through summer on plants with deeply lobed, glaucous gray-green leaves that look like a fancy ornamental poppy. It blooms over an exceptionally long season compared to most spring ephemerals and self-sows prolifically to form ever-expanding colonies in ideal conditions. The orange sap in the stems is a traditional dye plant but is caustic — wear gloves when dividing. Plants tolerate dry shade better than most spring wildflowers once established.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings or divisions in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring; yellow archangel spreads aggressively by stolons and should only be planted where its spread can be managed — it is invasive in some Pacific Northwest regions.

directions: Plant stem cuttings or divisions in moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade where spreading groundcover is desired; yellow archangel produces silvery-marked, semi-evergreen leaves year-round and bright yellow, hooded mint-family flowers in mid-spring that light up dark shade corners. It spreads vigorously by long, arching stolons that root where they touch the ground, forming a dense, weed-suppressing groundcover under established trees and shrubs within 1–2 seasons. Note: Lamiastrum galeobdolon 'Variegatum' and related cultivars are considered invasive in the Pacific Northwest and should not be planted where they can escape into natural areas — check local regulations before planting. Plant only in contained or managed sites in those regions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
Washington State Department of Agriculture — Invasive Species

planting.note: Plant small tuber divisions in early fall in deep, humus-rich, moist, well-drained, acidic soil in full shade under deciduous hardwoods; rue anemone is an extremely delicate spring ephemeral that requires undisturbed conditions to thrive.

directions: Plant tiny tuber clusters in early fall in deep, humus-rich, moist, well-drained, acidic soil in full shade under deciduous hardwoods, spacing 4–6 inches apart; rue anemone is one of the daintiest native spring ephemerals, producing wiry stems bearing small groups of rounded three-lobed leaves and 5–10-petaled white to pale pink flowers from early to mid-spring. The plant resembles both anemone and rue (Thalictrum) in appearance, hence its common name. It colonizes extremely slowly from tubers in ideal conditions but is long-lived and essentially permanent once established. Semi-double forms ('Shoaf's Double Pink') exist and are equally delicate. Plants go completely dormant by early summer — do not disturb in summer. Purchase only nursery-propagated stock.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; hairy toad lily is particularly valuable because it blooms in late summer and fall when few shade perennials are in flower.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade, spacing 18–24 inches apart; hairy toad lily blooms from late August through October when the shade garden is otherwise winding down, producing arching stems lined with lance-shaped leaves and studded along their upper portions with intricately spotted, orchid-like white-and-purple flowers that genuinely resemble miniature tropical orchids. The species name 'hirta' refers to the soft hairs covering stems and leaves. Plants spread gently by rhizomes to form loose colonies over time. Divide in early spring every 3–4 years to increase plantings; division is best done before new growth emerges. The closely related T. formosana is similar but tolerates zones 5–9 and tends to run more aggressively.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant small corms in early fall in deep, moist, well-drained, rich woodland soil in full shade under deciduous trees; spring beauty naturalizes by self-sowing and corm offsets into large, sweeping colonies over many years.

directions: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in early fall in moist, rich, well-drained woodland soil in full shade under deciduous hardwoods; spring beauty is one of the quintessential Eastern North American spring ephemerals, forming extensive carpets that produce thousands of small pink-striped white flowers in earliest spring before the tree canopy leafs out, then disappearing completely by late spring. The dime-sized corms are edible, with a starchy-sweet flavor resembling water chestnuts, and were an important food source for Eastern Native peoples. Plants naturalize slowly by both self-seeding and corm offsets to create breathtaking sweeps of spring color in undisturbed woodland gardens. Purchase nursery-propagated corms only.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
